source_metadata.title_raw: Lauren Langer - A-18-038 - March 15, 2018 - Manhattan Beach

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 15, 2018

Lauren Langer
Assistant City Attorney
City of West Hollywood
Jenkins & Hogin, LLP
1230 Rosecrans Avenue, Suite 110
Manhattan Beach, CA 90266

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-038

Dear Ms. Langer:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of West Hollywood City
Councilmember Lauren Meister regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform
Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest
provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common
law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Please also note that we are not a finder of fact when
rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your
facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit the Councilmember Meister from taking part in governmental
decisions relating to a multi-family residential development project that is within 472 feet of her
residence?

CONCLUSION

Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because they
would have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on her residence.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

You are the Assistant City Attorney for the City of West Hollywood and the authorized
representative of City Councilmember Meister. You provided additional facts on March 8, 2018.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-038
Page No. 2

The Councilmember owns her residence, a single-family home located on a residential street zoned for single family homes within the City. The Councilmember's residence is approximately 472 feet from a proposed development project located on La Cienega Boulevard within the City of Los Angeles. The project would demolish an existing car wash and replace it with a five-story residential apartment building with one subterranean level of parking.

Within 500 feet of the Councilmember's residence there is an existing multi-story multi-family residential building just south of the project, a 10-story hotel with nearly 300 rooms on Beverly Boulevard, and a preschool through 8th grade private school at the corner of Beverly Place and La Cienega. The Beverly Center, a large upscale shopping mall, is located on the corner of Beverly Boulevard and La Cienega, approximately 600 feet south of the Councilmember's residence. And Cedars-Sinai Medical Center, one of the largest nonprofit academic medical centers in the United States, is located on Beverly Boulevard, approximately 700 feet southeast of the Councilmember's residence.

The City of West Hollywood has no approval authority over the project. However, West Hollywood residents are concerned about potential impacts from the project on their properties and have asked the West Hollywood City Council to intervene in some manner to protect their interests. Accordingly, decisions relating to the project that may soon come before the West Hollywood City Council include all of the following:

• Whether to hire outside legal counsel to monitor the project;
• Whether to direct outside legal counsel and City of West Hollywood staff to provide written comment on environmental review or approval of the project;
• Whether City of West Hollywood officials should speak at public hearings before City of Los Angeles officials;
• Whether to appeal project approvals to the City of Los Angeles Planning Commission or City Council;
• Whether to take a formal position to support or oppose the project;
• Whether to contact City of Los Angeles officials to request or negotiate changes to the project;
• Whether to contact the developer to request or negotiate changes to the project; or
• Whether to file a lawsuit challenging project approvals by the City of Los Angeles.

ANALYSIS

A threshold issue exists regarding whether an official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act with respect to a development project located outside of the boundaries of the official's local governmental agency. Section 82035 sets forth the Act's definition of "jurisdiction," and provides that real property is within the jurisdiction of a local governmental agency if the property is located "not more than two miles outside the boundaries of the jurisdiction." The Act's definition of "jurisdiction" applies to its conflict of interest provisions. (Burton Advice Letter, No. A-07-052.) The project is less than two miles from the boundaries of the City of West Hollywood. Therefore, the project is within the City of West Hollywood's jurisdiction for purposes of the Act's conflict of interest provisions.

File No. A-18-038
Page No. 3

Sections 87100 and 87103 of the Act prohibit a public official from taking part in a
governmental decision that would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or
more of the official's financial interests. Section 87103 identifies the interests that may give rise to
an official's disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act. Assuming the Councilmember's
interest in her residence is worth $2,000 or more, she has a real property interest in her residence
under Section 87103(b).

Foreseeability and Materiality

For a financial interest that is not a named party in, or the subject of, the decision at issue,
the decision's financial effect on an official's financial interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can
be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." (Regulation
18701(b).) Because the Councilmember's real property interest in her residence is not a named
party in, or the subject of, the City of West Hollywood's potential decisions relating to the project,
those decisions' effect on the Councilmember's real property interest is reasonably foreseeable if it
can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical.

As set forth in Regulation 18702.2(a)(11), if a decision involves property within 500 feet of
the official's real property interest, the decision's effect on the official's interest is material unless
there are sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable impact
on the official's property. The project would increase the intensity of use, traffic, and demand for
public parking in the area surrounding the Councilmember's residence. The potential decisions that
may come before the West Hollywood City Council regarding the project may delay, prevent, or
substantially modify the project. Therefore, those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable
measurable impact on the Councilmember's real property interest in her residence. Accordingly, the
Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in decisions relating to the project.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Assistant General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl
